title: Bootstrap bounds for Quantum Spin Systems using String Operators
authors: Nisarg Chadha, Michael G. Scheer, Eslam Khalaf
abstract: Bootstrap is a numerical many-body method that provides rigorous bounds on ground-state observables by imposing a set of necessary constraints on the expectation values of operators. The quality of the resulting bounds is sensitive to the choice of operators entering the constraints. In particular, bounds on ground-state correlations are often loose in spontaneous symmetry-breaking (SSB) phases, since local operator sets cannot exclude domain-wall excitations. In this work, we introduce non-local, string-like operators into the bootstrap and show that the program can be formulated directly in thermodynamic limit. We then apply our construction to several 1D spin models. First, we obtain a significant tightening of the bounds in the SSB phase of the 1D transverse-field Ising model. Using the 1D axial next-nearest-neighbor Ising model, we further show that this tightening allows for a quantitative estimate of the locations of phase boundaries. Finally, we generalize the string operators to the 1D $\mathbb{Z}_3$ chiral clock model and track the behavior of the bounds across the phase diagram. Our results broaden the class of constraints available to the bootstrap and open a route toward bootstrapping more general symmetry-broken and topological phases, where the relevant constraints may involve non-local or extended operators.
summary: This paper advances the many-body bootstrap method by introducing non-local 'string' operators to improve bounds on ground-state observables in quantum spin systems. By applying this technique to models like the TFIM and ANNNI, the authors demonstrate that these extended constraints can accurately map out phase boundaries and resolve issues related to domain-wall excitations in symmetry-broken phases.
main_problem: The primary goal is obtaining rigorous, tight bounds on ground-state observables for quantum spin systems in the thermodynamic limit.
main_result: By incorporating non-local, string-like operators into the bootstrap method, the authors significantly tighten existing bounds, especially in spontaneously symmetry-breaking (SSB) phases.
method: They employ the many-body bootstrap technique, formulating it as a Semidefinite Program (SDP), and crucially extend the operator set with non-local 'string' operators to handle domain-wall excitations.
model_or_system: The work applies to 1D quantum spin systems, including the transverse-field Ising model (TFIM), the axial next-nearest-neighbor Ising model (ANNNI), and the Z3 chiral clock model. The Hamiltonians describe various magnetic ordering phases.
key_observables: Ground-state observables, energy density ($\Delta E$), magnetization density, and correlation functions ($\langle Z_0 Z_r angle$).
important_parameters: The transverse field strength ($g$), the next-nearest-neighbor coupling ($\kappa$), and the symmetry parameter ($\phi$) are key tuning parameters used to map out phase diagrams.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that incorporating string operators allows for a formulation directly in the thermodynamic limit, overcoming limitations of local operator sets in SSB phases.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the tightening of bootstrap bounds (e.g., energy density vs. parameters) when comparing results from local versus string-operator constrained SDPs across different models like TFIM and ANNNI.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the limitations of local operators in SSB phases, then introduces non-local string operators to formulate the bootstrap in the thermodynamic limit. It applies this construction sequentially to 1D TFIM, then ANNNI, and finally generalizes it to the Z3 chiral clock model.
why_interesting: The development of methods that rigorously constrain ground state properties using extended, non-local operators is highly valuable for understanding quantum phase transitions in strongly correlated magnetic materials where simple local descriptions fail.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic showing a particular arrangement of spins which has a domain wall across the bond between i and i + 1. The action of O on this state removes the domain wall at i, and produces a state with lower energy for the Ising Hamiltonian when g = 0.

FIG. 2. Bootstrap bounds for the 1D TFIM in the thermody- namic limit using string operators. (a) Bounds on the energy density as a function of transverse field, with and without strings. The corresponding operator sets are chosen to be of the same size for a fair comparison. The inset shows the rela- tive difference of the upper and lower bounds with respect to the exact energy density. (b) Bounds on the magnetization density as a function of transverse field. The bounds are sym- metric about the origin due to the Z2 symmetry. (c) Bounds on the ⟨Z0Z1⟩correlations as a function of transverse field. (d) Bounds on the long-range correlation ⟨Z0Zr⟩as a func- tion of distance r. Tight bounds...

FIG. 3. (a) Schematic phase diagram of the 1D ANNNI. (b) Uncertainty (difference between upper bound and lower bound) in energy density in the g −κ plane. The dots rep- resent the peaks extracted from ∆E −g cuts at different κ. The dashed line shows the Peschel-Emery disorder line where the Hamiltonian becomes frustration-free. (c) ∆E −g cuts at different κ ≤0.5. The inset shows the corresponding cuts for κ ≥0.5 (d) Bounds on the correlation function ⟨Z0Zr⟩as a function of transverse field at two values of κ. The κ = 0.2 line passes through the FM-PM boundary, whereas the κ = 0.4 profile also goes through the floating phase.

FIG. 4. Bootstrap bounds for the 1D CC3 in the thermodynamic limit using the string operators. (a) Bounds on the energy density as a function of transverse field, with and without strings. The corresponding operator sets are chosen to be of the same size for a fair comparison. The inset shows the difference between the upper and lower bounds versus the transverse field. (b) Bounds on the correlation functions ⟨Z0Z† r + Z† 0Zr⟩/2 as a function of distance for g = 0.4 and g = 2.0. (c) and (d) Bounds on the energy density and the nearest neighbor correlation g(1) as a function of ϕ for g = 0.
